title: Advanced concessive and contrast structures
lesson: Formal English offers a rich set of concessive structures that go well beyond 'although'. 'Much as I admire her' fronts the verb of feeling for emphasis ('Much as + subject + verb'). 'However much/hard + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of effort changes the outcome. 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' ('For all his talent, he failed'). 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point before countering it. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, typically an adjective or adverbial, not a full finite clause. 'Notwithstanding' functions as a preposition ('notwithstanding the objections') or, more formally, follows its noun. These patterns differ chiefly in what must follow them, which is what tells the right connector apart from its near-synonyms.

items.text: {1} I admire her courage, I simply cannot support such a reckless decision.

blanks.hint: fronts a verb of feeling: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much as I admire her courage' is the fixed concessive pattern 'Much as + subject + verb' meaning 'although I greatly admire'. 'As much' inverts the words and is ungrammatical here; 'Despite' must be followed by a noun or -ing, not a finite clause; 'However' would need an adjective/adverb ('However much').

items.text: {1} hard she tried, she could never quite master the pronunciation.

blanks.hint: ___ + adverb + subject + verb = no degree of effort helped

items.explanation: 'However + adverb (hard) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of trying changed the result. 'Much as' takes a verb directly, not an adverb; 'Whatever' takes a noun/clause, not the adverb 'hard'; 'Albeit' cannot introduce a finite clause like 'she tried'.

items.text: {1} his undeniable talent, he never achieved the recognition he deserved.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of' / 'despite': 'For all his talent' = 'despite his talent'. 'Much as' and 'However' require a following clause/adjective, not a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase that qualifies something already stated, not a fronted 'in spite of' phrase.

items.text: {1} that the proposal has merit, we still lack the funds to implement it this year.

blanks.hint: concede a point before countering: ___ (that) + clause

items.explanation: 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point you are about to argue against: 'Granted that the proposal has merit, we still...'. 'Albeit' cannot take a 'that'-clause; 'Despite' takes a noun phrase or -ing, never a finite 'that'-clause; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb ('Much as the proposal has merit'), not by 'that'.

items.text: The committee approved the revised budget, {1} with considerable reluctance.

blanks.hint: introduces a concessive phrase (no finite verb), here 'with reluctance'

items.explanation: 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase qualifying what precedes it: 'approved..., albeit with considerable reluctance'. The other options all require a following clause or different structure ('much as'/'granted' need a subject and verb; 'however' would need an adjective/adverb), so none fits a bare prepositional phrase.

items.text: {1} the strenuous objections of the board, the merger went ahead as planned.

blanks.hint: a formal preposition: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' is a formal preposition meaning 'in spite of', so it is followed directly by the noun phrase 'the strenuous objections'. 'Much as' requires a subject + verb ('Much as the board objected'); 'However' needs a following adjective/adverb; 'Even though' introduces a finite clause, not a bare noun phrase.

items.text: Hard {1} he tried, he never managed to win her approval.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective/adverb + ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: The fronted concessive pattern 'Adjective/Adverb + though + subject + verb' ('Hard though he tried') is an emphatic equivalent of 'although he tried hard'. 'Despite' takes a noun/-ing, not a clause; 'however' would itself stand at the front ('However hard he tried'), not after the fronted word; 'much' does not link a fronted adverb to a clause in this slot.

items.text: Exhausted {1} she was, she insisted on finishing the marathon.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + be = concessive 'as ... as'

items.explanation: 'Adjective + as + subject + be' ('Exhausted as she was') is a concessive inversion meaning 'although she was exhausted', common in formal and literary English. 'Though as' doubles the connector and is ungrammatical; 'however' would precede the adjective ('However exhausted she was'); 'despite' cannot govern the finite clause 'she was'.

items.text: The plan is undeniably bold. {1}, I remain convinced it can be made to work.

blanks.hint: a conjunct linking back to the previous sentence: 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Even so' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of that', linking back to the preceding sentence and standing alone before a comma. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause within the same sentence; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} the figures may look discouraging, they conceal a genuine improvement in efficiency.

blanks.hint: subordinator used concessively: ___ + finite clause = although

items.explanation: 'While' (or 'Whilst') used concessively introduces a finite clause and means 'although': 'While the figures may look discouraging'. 'For all' and 'Notwithstanding' are followed by a noun phrase, not a finite clause; 'Albeit' cannot head a full finite clause with its own subject and modal ('the figures may look').

items.text: The treatment proved effective, {1} slower to take hold than the doctors had hoped.

blanks.hint: introduces a concessive phrase (here an adjective), not a finite clause

items.explanation: 'Albeit' qualifies a preceding statement with a concessive adjective or adverbial phrase: 'effective, albeit slower to take hold'. 'Even so' is a free-standing conjunct that links whole sentences and would need its own main clause; 'Much as' and 'While' both require a following finite clause with a subject and verb, not the bare adjective phrase 'slower to take hold'.

items.text: {1} though I respect his experience, I cannot accept his conclusion on this point.

blanks.hint: intensifier + 'though' + subject + verb of feeling

items.explanation: 'Much though + subject + verb' is an emphatic concessive equivalent of 'Much as I respect his experience' / 'although I greatly respect'. The slot needs the intensifier 'Much' before 'though'. 'However' would replace 'though' and take an adjective/adverb, not precede it; 'Despite' takes a noun or -ing, not 'though + clause'; 'As' would produce 'As though', which means 'as if', not concession.

items.text: {1} the fact that costs had tripled, the council refused to cancel the festival.

blanks.hint: preposition phrase + 'the fact that' + clause = despite

items.explanation: 'In spite of' is a prepositional phrase, so to govern a finite clause it must take the noun 'the fact' followed by a 'that'-clause: 'In spite of the fact that costs had tripled'. 'Even though' introduces a finite clause directly and cannot stand before 'the fact that' here ('Even though the fact that...' is ungrammatical); 'Much as' takes subject + verb, not 'the fact that'; 'Albeit' introduces a phrase, not 'the fact that' + clause.

items.text: {1} as it may seem, the quietest applicant turned out to be the most demanding negotiator.

blanks.hint: bare adjective + 'as it may seem' = though it seems strange

items.explanation: 'Strange as it may seem' is a concessive inversion of the form 'adjective + as + subject + may + seem', meaning 'although it may seem strange'. The slot requires the bare adjective 'Strange' because 'seem' is a copular verb that takes an adjective complement, so the adverb 'Strangely' is wrong; 'However' would need to precede the adjective and drop 'as' ('However strange it may seem'); 'Albeit' introduces a phrase, not this inverted clause with its own subject and modal.

items.text: The northern branch posted record profits this quarter, {1} the southern branch barely broke even.

blanks.hint: subordinator of direct contrast between two clauses: 'while by contrast'

items.explanation: 'Whereas' is a subordinator of direct contrast, introducing a finite clause that is set against the main clause: '..., whereas the southern branch barely broke even'. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a full clause with its own subject and verb; 'Notwithstanding' is a preposition taking a noun phrase ('notwithstanding the figures'); 'Much as' is followed by a verb of feeling and means 'although', not the balanced 'by contrast' relationship needed here.

items.text: {1} reservations he may privately harbour, in public he supports the policy without question.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun + subject + 'may' + verb = no matter what

items.explanation: 'Whatever + noun + subject + may + verb' is a concessive pattern meaning 'no matter what', here governing the noun 'reservations' ('Whatever reservations he may harbour'). 'However' would take an adjective/adverb, not the noun 'reservations'; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject and modal; 'Much as' is followed directly by a verb of feeling, not by a noun object.

items.text: I see no reason to change course now. {1}, I am willing to review the decision in six months.

blanks.hint: a conjunct qualifying one's own previous statement: 'having said that'

items.explanation: 'That said' is a fixed conjunct ('having said that') used to introduce a qualification or partial concession to one's own preceding statement, standing alone before a comma. 'Said that' reverses the fixed order and is ungrammatical here; 'Much as' needs a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a main clause.

items.text: The evidence against him was overwhelming. {1}, the jury chose to acquit.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'For all that' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / nevertheless', linking back to the preceding sentence and standing alone before a comma. It differs from 'For all + noun phrase' ('For all his talent'): 'For all' alone cannot stand before a comma without a following noun; 'Much as' demands a following subject + verb; 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: {1} I am to criticise a colleague, I cannot let this error pass unremarked.

blanks.hint: fronted adjective + ___ + subject + be = 'although I am reluctant'

items.explanation: The pattern 'Adjective + as + subject + be' ('Loath as I am') fronts the adjective for an emphatic concession meaning 'although I am reluctant'. 'Loath that' is ungrammatical, as 'that' cannot link a fronted predicative adjective to its clause; 'However loath' would require no second connector and a different word order ('However loath I am'); 'Much as' takes a verb of feeling, not the predicative adjective 'loath'.

items.text: {1} late it was, the shops on the main square were still crowded with tourists.

blanks.hint: ___ + adjective + subject + verb = 'regardless of how'

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adjective/adverb + subject + verb' means 'regardless of how': 'No matter how late it was'. 'Much as' takes a verb directly, not the adjective 'late'; 'For all' takes a bare noun phrase; 'Albeit' cannot head a finite clause with its own subject ('it was').

items.text: {1} the risks involved, the surgeon judged that operating was the only realistic option.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of': 'For all the risks involved' = 'despite the risks'. 'Even though' and 'While' introduce a finite clause, not a bare noun phrase; 'Much as' must be followed by a subject and verb ('Much as the risks worried her'), not by the bare noun phrase 'the risks involved'.

items.text: {1}, he could not persuade the rusty lock to open.

blanks.hint: fronted bare verb + as + subject + modal = however hard he tried

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is the fronted-verb concessive inversion 'Verb + as + subject + might/would', meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'Much as he might' and 'However he might' leave the modal 'might' with no infinitive to complete it and no adverb after 'However', so both are incomplete; 'As much he might' simply scrambles the word order and is ungrammatical.

items.text: {1} as they might, the investigators could not establish how the intruder had entered the building.

blanks.hint: fixed idiom: ___ as + subject + might/would = however hard they tried

items.explanation: 'Try as they might' is a fixed concessive idiom — fronted bare verb + 'as' + subject + 'might/would' — meaning 'however hard they tried'. Since the modal 'might' already follows the subject, the fronted slot must hold the lexical verb 'Try'. 'Hard' and 'Much' are an adverb and a quantifier that would each still need a following verb ('Hard as they might try', 'Much as they tried'), so neither completes 'as they might'; 'Even as they might' is temporal or ungrammatical, not concessive.

items.text: The board's reservations {1}, the chief executive pushed the acquisition through.

blanks.hint: used post-nominally, after its noun phrase: [noun phrase] ___, ... = in spite of

items.explanation: Here 'notwithstanding' is used post-nominally: it follows the noun phrase it governs ('The board's reservations notwithstanding'), a formal alternative to the fronted 'Notwithstanding the board's reservations'. 'Despite' is also a preposition but can only precede its noun, never follow it; 'although' is a subordinating conjunction that must introduce a finite clause, not sit after a bare noun phrase; 'however' requires a following adjective or adverb and an altogether different structure.

items.text: {1} of his proposal, the timing could hardly have been worse.

blanks.hint: elliptical concession: ___ [may be] + 'of' + noun

items.explanation: 'Whatever the merits (of his proposal)' is an elliptical concessive clause — 'whatever the merits may be' — conceding the point regardless of how the argument is judged, and is uniquely able to be followed by 'of + noun'. 'However' needs a following adjective or adverb, not 'of'; 'Much as' requires a subject + finite verb; 'Even though' must introduce a complete finite clause, not the fragment 'of his proposal'.

items.text: The prototype exceeded every performance target. {1}, the board still refused to fund full production.

blanks.hint: a fixed conjunct standing alone before a comma: 'even so / nevertheless'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive conjunct meaning 'even so / nevertheless'; it stands complete before a comma and is followed by an independent main clause. The distractors cannot stand alone in this slot: 'Even though' is a subordinator that must introduce its own finite clause, not sit isolated before a comma and a separate main clause; 'For all' requires a following noun phrase ('For all its success'); 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb ('Much as it impressed us').

items.text: The pilot scheme was scrapped after only six months, {1} it had exceeded every target set for it.

blanks.hint: lets a preposition govern a finite clause: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Despite the fact that' allows the preposition 'despite' to govern a finite clause: the noun 'the fact' serves as its object and 'that + clause' completes it, giving 'despite the fact that it had exceeded every target'. Bare 'despite' cannot be followed directly by a finite clause ('despite it had exceeded'); 'notwithstanding' likewise takes a noun phrase, not a subject + verb; 'albeit' introduces only a concessive phrase (an adjective or adverbial), not a full clause with its own subject and verb.

items.text: {1} it should cost me my job, I intend to make these findings public.

blanks.hint: introduces a hypothetical concession (it may or may not happen) + finite clause

items.explanation: 'Even if' introduces a hypothetical, as-yet-unrealised concession: the speaker does not know whether it will cost the job, only that the decision stands regardless. 'Even though' would assert the cost as an established fact, contradicting the hypothetical 'should cost'; 'Much as' needs a verb of feeling and a plain subject + verb ('Much as I value my job'); 'Notwithstanding' is a preposition and must govern a noun phrase ('Notwithstanding the risk'), not a finite clause.

items.text: I have made my decision, and I shall stand by it, {1}.

blanks.hint: a fixed end-placed idiom = 'whatever happens'

items.explanation: 'Come what may' is a fixed universal-concessive idiom meaning 'whatever happens', naturally placed at the end after a comma. 'Even so' is a conjunct that introduces its own main clause ('Even so, I shall stand by it'), not a trailing tag; 'Albeit' must be followed by a concessive phrase (an adjective or prepositional phrase), not left dangling; 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb and cannot close the sentence.

items.text: {1} tempting the offer may seem, accepting it would compromise our independence.

blanks.hint: ___ + adjective + subject + verb = to whatever degree

items.explanation: 'However + adjective (tempting) + subject + verb' concedes that no degree of the quality changes the outcome ('no matter how tempting the offer seems'). This adjective-fronting slot is exactly where the near-synonyms fail: 'Much as' is followed directly by a verb of feeling ('Much as the offer tempts us'), not by an adjective; 'For all' governs a noun phrase ('For all its appeal'); and 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a finite clause with its own subject and modal ('the offer may seem').

items.text: {1} I sympathise with their predicament, I cannot in good conscience waive the rules for them.

blanks.hint: fronts a verb of feeling: ___ + subject + verb

items.explanation: 'Much as + subject + verb' fronts a verb of feeling for emphasis and means 'although I greatly sympathise'. The distractors differ precisely in what must follow: 'For all' requires a noun phrase ('For all my sympathy'); 'However' requires a following adjective or adverb ('However much I sympathise'), not a bare verb; and 'Despite' is a preposition taking a noun or -ing form, so it cannot govern the finite clause 'I sympathise'.

items.text: {1} its impressive specifications on paper, the device proved hopelessly unreliable in daily use.

blanks.hint: ___ + noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'For all + noun phrase' means 'in spite of': 'For all its impressive specifications' = 'despite its specifications'. 'Much as' requires a following subject + verb, not a bare noun phrase; 'However' needs a following adjective/adverb ('However impressive'); 'Granted' introduces a conceded clause ('Granted that its specifications are impressive'), not a fronted noun phrase.

items.text: {1} much he earns, he never seems to have enough to clear his debts.

blanks.hint: ___ + much + subject + verb = no amount changes the outcome

items.explanation: 'However + much + subject + verb' concedes that no amount of something (here, earning) alters the result: 'However much he earns'. 'Much as' is followed directly by the verb of feeling ('Much as I admire'), not by the quantifier 'much'; 'For all' requires a noun phrase ('For all his earnings'), not 'much + clause'; 'Whatever' cannot precede 'much' in this slot, since 'Whatever much he earns' is ungrammatical.

items.text: Financial constraints {1}, the museum somehow managed to acquire the entire private collection.

blanks.hint: a formal preposition placed AFTER its noun phrase = in spite of

items.explanation: 'Notwithstanding' may follow the noun phrase it governs ('Financial constraints notwithstanding' = 'in spite of the financial constraints'), a formal, semi-legal postposed usage. 'Albeit' introduces a concessive phrase and cannot mean 'in spite of' after a noun; 'however' needs a following adjective or adverb; 'for all' must precede its noun phrase ('For all the financial constraints'), never follow it.

items.text: {1} that as it may, the underlying problem has still not been addressed.

blanks.hint: fixed subjunctive formula: ___ that as it may = 'granting that may be so'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fixed concessive formula built on the fronted subjunctive 'be', meaning 'even though that may be true'; it stands alone before a comma and a full main clause. 'However' requires a following adjective/adverb or a different clause structure; 'Even' would need a partner word ('Even so', 'Even though') and cannot head 'that as it may'; 'Much' needs 'as + subject + verb' and cannot fill the subjunctive slot of this frozen phrase.

items.text: The panel dismissed the design as impractical. {1}, the client adopted it and it won a national award.

blanks.hint: fixed subjunctive concessive conjunct = 'even granting that is so'

items.explanation: 'Be that as it may' is a fossilised subjunctive conjunct meaning 'even if that is true', standing complete before a comma and looking back to the previous sentence. 'Be as it may' drops the obligatory pronoun 'that', which is the subject of the fronted subjunctive 'be'; the idiom is fixed and cannot be reduced, so the truncation is ungrammatical. 'Much as it may' requires a following lexical verb ('Much as it may seem impractical'); left with the bare modal 'may' it is incomplete and cannot function as a free-standing conjunct before a full clause. 'Even though that' opens a subordinate clause that needs a subject and finite verb after it; a bare demonstrative 'that' with no verb cannot stand alone before a comma as a concessive linker.

items.text: I've decided to sell the car. {1} I ever really needed it, but the running costs have simply become too high.

blanks.hint: qualifying afterthought: ___ + clause = 'it's not as if'

items.explanation: 'Not that + clause' is a concessive qualifier meaning 'it is not the case / not as if': 'Not that I ever really needed it' concedes a point in passing. 'Much as' needs a positive verb of feeling directly after it; 'Albeit' cannot head a full finite clause with its own subject; 'Granted' would concede that I DID need the car, contradicting the intended 'not' meaning.

items.text: {1} that the evidence is largely circumstantial, it nonetheless points firmly in one direction.

blanks.hint: concede a point before countering: ___ (that) + clause

items.explanation: 'Granted (that) + clause' concedes a point one is about to argue against: 'Granted that the evidence is circumstantial, it nonetheless...'. 'Albeit' cannot take a 'that'-clause; 'Despite' governs a noun phrase or -ing form, never a finite 'that'-clause; 'Much as' is followed directly by subject + verb ('Much as the evidence is circumstantial'), not by 'that'.

items.text: The evidence against him was largely circumstantial. The jury, {1}, returned a verdict of guilty.

blanks.hint: parenthetical conjunct between commas = 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Nonetheless' (like 'nevertheless') is a conjunct meaning 'in spite of that / even so'; it can sit parenthetically between commas inside the clause and points back to the previous sentence. 'Even though' and 'much as' must each introduce their own subordinate clause with a subject and verb, which the bare parenthetical slot does not provide; 'albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing sentence connector.

items.text: The setback did nothing to dampen their enthusiasm; {1}, it only hardened their resolve.

blanks.hint: a conjunct conceding the opposite tendency: 'on the contrary, to some degree'

items.explanation: 'If anything' is a concessive conjunct meaning 'on the contrary, if there was any effect at all', used to correct an expectation in the opposite direction: far from dampening enthusiasm, the setback strengthened it. 'Even though' must introduce a subordinate clause, not stand alone between clauses; 'much as' requires a following subject and verb; 'albeit' introduces a concessive phrase, not a free-standing connector before a full main clause.

items.text: Her arguments were widely regarded as flawed. {1}, they carried the day at the meeting.

blanks.hint: stand-alone conjunct linking back: 'in spite of that'

items.explanation: 'Nevertheless' is a sentence conjunct meaning 'in spite of that'; it stands alone before a comma and a full main clause, referring back to the previous sentence. 'Even though' is a subordinator that must introduce a dependent clause within its own sentence, not stand alone; 'Much as' needs a following subject and verb; 'Whereas' is a subordinating conjunction of contrast and likewise cannot function as a free-standing conjunct before a comma.

items.text: {1} persuasive his rhetoric, the facts stubbornly refused to bear out his conclusion.

blanks.hint: ___ + adjective + subject (+ verb) = 'to whatever degree'

items.explanation: 'No matter how + adjective + subject' ('No matter how persuasive his rhetoric [was]') concedes that no degree of the quality changes the result. 'However much' modifies quantity or a verb, not a predicative adjective ('However much persuasive' is ungrammatical); 'Whatever' governs a noun, not the adjective 'persuasive'; 'No matter that' takes a full finite clause ('No matter that his rhetoric was persuasive'), so it cannot precede the bare 'persuasive his rhetoric'.

items.text: {1} he might, he could not rid himself of the nagging suspicion that he had been deceived.

blanks.hint: fixed concessive idiom: ___ + subject + 'might/may' = no matter how hard one tries

items.explanation: 'Try as he might' is a fixed concessive idiom built on the pattern 'Try as + subject + might/may', meaning 'however hard he tried'. 'However' would need a following adjective/adverb ('However hard he tried') and cannot precede the bare 'he might'; 'Much as' is followed by a verb of feeling ('Much as he tried'), not the modal-final 'he might'; 'Even though' introduces an ordinary finite clause and does not license the fronted 'might'.
